In that case, the price, market capitalization and index weight of A will advance, and in equilibrium, the extra return provided by A in this self-fulfilling bubble will be exactly the “edge” that investors imagine should exist. It’s not “money flow” that does the job. It’s this “perceived edge” that makes the weight increase.
From this perspective, it’s straightforward to explain a world where the index weights of individual stocks are “forced” higher. It’s a world where investors come to believe that certain stocks have a built-in performance edge, beyond the expected return of the market itself. I think that’s precisely the world we’re living in at the moment. It’s the shared belief in a performance edge that drives the stock’s market weighting higher. The prices are held up by preferences and expectations.
Nowhere is the “increasing weight” story more evident than in the technology sector. The chart below shows valuation of the S&P Information Technology index, based on price/revenue.

How do we know this trajectory isn’t just rational valuation at work? Well, one of the red flags is that, for the largest technology stocks, valuation multiples haven’t declined as enormous growth in fundamentals has proceeded. Despite profound revenue growth since 2000, many of the behemoths currently have price/revenue multiples close to or higher than the multiples they had in the late-1990’s.
Consider a company that’s expected to grow revenues and earnings at a very high rate over an extended period of time, before slowing toward the growth rate of the overall economy (we know that growth must eventually slow to a growth rate at or below the rate of economic growth, or the company would eventually represent 100% of GDP).
Well, unless investors collectively believe that the stock should provide a long-term return that’s even higher than the high-growth rate of fundamentals, valuation multiples should progressively decline during the high-growth phase. Suppose for example that fundamentals have doubled over the past year. Even if the stock was priced to provide 20% long-term annual returns, if price goes up by 20% as revenues and earnings double, the valuation multiple falls to 1.2/2.0 = 0.6 of what it was a year ago. As it should.
If valuations aren’t falling, one of two things (or both) must be occurring from a present-value perspective. Either there has been a significant decline in the long-term rate of return investors can expect, or investors are repeatedly shifting the entire trajectory of future growth higher – treating each period as a new starting point, but pushing the same high expected growth rate further and further into the future.
In my view, both of these are occurring. Specifically, valuations for the largest information technology companies are at levels that only make sense if one discounts optimistic future cash flows at depressed long-term rates of return. Meanwhile, these valuations suggest that the expected returns in the heads of investors are tethered to the current earnings and revenue growth rates of the companies themselves, as if these growth rates are permanent.
When investors think about the “edge” that a particular stock may have, it’s important to distinguish between an “edge” measured simply based on a backward-looking increase in price, and an “edge” based on a high expected future long-term investment return – driven by valuations that relate the current price with expected future cash flows. Often, investors imagine that stocks with very high past returns and explosive past growth in revenues and earnings obviously have an “edge” – believing that this past behavior frees them from having to examine valuations.
That’s the fatal error that investors commit in every speculative bubble. It’s exactly why we projected the scope for an 83% loss in technology stocks at the March 2000 market peak. From the 2000 peak to the October 2002 trough, the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 followed just that trajectory – losing 50% of its value, followed by a loss of 50% of what remained, followed by the loss of nearly a third of what was left by the time the market bottomed.
Recall the largest three tech stocks at the end of 1999 by market capitalization: Microsoft, Cisco, and Intel. Their total returns, respectively, since 1999 have averaged 9.6%, 3.2%, and 2.6%. The average is 5.1%. Seeing the largest companies in each market cycle go on to lag, as a group, is the norm, not the exception.
As I observed in 2024 (see The Turtle and the Pendulum), high growth rates and profit margins aren’t fixed numbers. They’re trajectories. When investors value stocks based on extrapolation of the growth phase, they put a pendulum on their turtle and swing it all the way forward. The fact is that except during the speculative blowoffs that drive them to an extreme market share (e.g. 1929, 1972, 2000, today), the largest cap stocks tend to follow by underperforming the average stock over time.
Bubbles are generated when investors drive valuations higher without simultaneously adjusting expectations for future returns lower. In other words, the defining feature of a bubble is inconsistency between expected returns based on price behavior and expected returns based on valuations. The ‘Bubble Term’ measures the gap between the two.
Unless the Bubble Term is able to become exponentially larger forever – it shows up as a growing gap between the long-term return that investors expect in their heads, and the long-term return that investors can actually expect based on the future cash flows that will ultimately be delivered into their hands.
Sustaining the bubble requires valuation multiples to increase forever, without any upper bound. The reason the current bubble feels so good to investors is that, up until the present moment, valuation multiples have done exactly that.
– John P. Hussman, Ph.D, The Bubble Term, August 14, 2025
As Nobel laureate Franco Modigliani observed in March 2000, as the tech bubble was peaking, “The bubble is rational in a certain sense. The expectation of growth produces the growth, which confirms the expectation; people will buy it because it went up. But once you are convinced that it is not growing anymore, nobody wants to hold a stock because it is overvalued. Everybody wants to get out and it collapses, beyond the fundamentals.”
A few weeks earlier, on March 7, 2000, I observed “Over time, price/revenue ratios come back in line. Currently, that would require an 83% plunge in tech stocks (recall the 1969-70 tech massacre). If you understand values and market history, you know we’re not joking.” By October 2002, the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 had lost precisely that amount of its market capitalization.
As valuations are driven higher, and true long-term expected returns – implied by discounted cash flows – are driven lower, my impression is that there is a widening gap between the expected returns that investors have in their heads and that actual returns that can be expected based on likely future cash flows. The effect is particularly amplified in the information technology sector.
The upshot is this. The U.S. equity markets stand at the most extreme point of valuation in U.S. financial market history. Record deficits in the government and household sectors have a mirror image that is observed as record corporate profits. While the distribution of these profits has benefited large technology companies that enjoy enormous network effects (a public good for which the public is not compensated), the level of these profits reflects a macroeconomic structure that taxes wage and salary income at vastly higher rates than it taxes income from securities and dividends, and leaves tens of trillions of dollars of capital gains wholly untaxed because they can be deferred without limit.
Meanwhile, investors have interpreted these profits as permanent, and are assigning record or near-record valuation multiples to these profits, generating a speculative bubble. At a sector level, the largest companies – particularly in information technology – are being priced as if they have a fixed “performance edge” regardless of valuations. This has produced a bubble upon a bubble, with a likely outcome not entirely different than the 2000-2002 collapse, in which the technology-heavy Nasdaq 100 index lost 83% of its value from peak-to-trough.
It stands repeating, again and again, that nothing in our investment discipline relies on a market collapse, or a long-term reversion in valuations. We’re certainly aware of the risks, and at current extremes, there’s no situation in which we would operate without a safety net against major market losses. At the same time, we’ve significantly adapted the implementation of our investment discipline – not only by increasing our ability to vary the intensity of “bearish” investment positions in September 2024 (see the section titled “The Martian” in The Turtle and the Pendulum) but more recently, by expanding our ability to periodically reverse our hedges from mildly bearish to “constructive with a safety net” – removing the “upside cap” on potential returns, even amid extreme valuations (see How I Learned to Love the Bubble).
I expect that my views will continue to sound “bearish” to the indiscriminate observer, because I write only once a month, and our hedging variations tend to be short-term in nature – even though they’ve allowed us to benefit from a generally rising market since September 2024, despite that generally “bearish” outlook. What’s important is to recognize that our views aren’t “forecasts” and we don’t require any particular market outcome. Our discipline is to consistently align our investment stance with objective, measurable, observable market conditions as they change over time. The best way to take good care of the future is to repeatedly take good care of the present moment.
Geek’s Note: Active investment weights reflect perceived “edge”
To provide some additional intuition on how investor perceptions are related to the active and passive weights that investors choose, here’s a derivation of a classic asset pricing approach that I used to teach my undergraduates at the University of Michigan. Emphatically, we don’t have to “believe” the “Capital Asset Pricing Model” itself. Rather, what’s important is the underlying concept: the weight that investors place on any given stock is a reflection of the risk-adjusted “edge” that they expect for that stock.
Suppose an investor can allocate investment funds across a risk-free investment earning Rf, some individual stock A with uncertain return Ra, the market portfolio M with uncertain return Rm.
Define the portfolio weights on these assets as wf,wm,wa with
wf+wm+wa=1.
The portfolio return can be written as
Rp=Rf+wa(Ra–Rf)+wm(Rm–Rf).
Its expected return is
E[Rp]=Rf+wa(E[Ra]–Rf)+wm(E[Rm]–Rf).
Now consider the risk of the portfolio. Writing
εa=Ra–E[Ra],
εm=Rm–E[Rm],
and using E[εa]=E[εm]=0, the random part of the portfolio return (actual minus expected) is
Rp–E[Rp]=wmεm+waεa,
so the portfolio variance Var(Rp) is
σp2=wa2σa2+wm2σm2+2wawmσa,m,
where
σa2=Var(Ra),
σm2=Var(Rm),
σa,m=Cov(Ra,Rm).
Now suppose an investor chooses portfolio weights in order to maximize expected return for a given level of risk σ¯2. We can write the Lagrangian
L=E[Rp]+λ(σ¯2–Var(Rp)),
with multiplier λ>0. Using our expressions for expected return and risk, taking derivatives with respect to wa and wm, and setting them to zero gives these first-order conditions
E[Ra]–Rf=λ(2waσa2+2wmσa,m),
E[Rm]–Rf=λ(2wmσm2+2waσa,m).
The Lagrange multiplier λ reflects how much expected return the investor requires as compensation for a one-unit increase in portfolio variance.
You’ll sometimes see the investor’s “objective function” written as the problem of maximizing expected return minus a risk penalty: γ/2 times portfolio variance, where γ is a measure of risk-aversion. That’s actually equivalent to our Lagrangian, and simply substitutes λ=γ/2. The 2s that appear when taking the first derivative then vanish, leaving equations in γ alone.
Now, suppose we define the “market portfolio” broadly enough that every stock is represented. By definition, all stocks must be held – taking all investors together, their portfolio holdings will add up to “the market.” In equilibrium, this means that no stock can ever be “underowned’ or “overowned.”
Suppose, for simplicity, that investors share the same views about expected returns. What must be true about expected returns in order for investors to choose no additional active position in A beyond the weight it already has inside the market portfolio?
If the investor chooses wa=0, the first-order conditions must reduce to
E[Rm]–Rf=2λwmσm2,E[Ra]–Rf=2λwmσa,m.
Eliminating the common factor 2λwm yields
E[Ra]–Rfσa,m=E[Rm]–Rfσm2.
Finally, we can define βA as the “beta” of stock A. For clarity, this is a different beta than the “bubble term” in my August 2025 comment, and in the Geek’s note that follows. Here, beta is defined as percentage change in stock A in response to a 1% change in the market. In other words, βA measures how much market risk “loads” onto stock A. Statistically, βA is defined as the covariance between the returns of stock A and the market, divided by the variance of market returns:
βA=Cov(Ra,Rm)Var(Rm)=σa,mσm2.
Substituting this definition of βA into the optimality conditions, we find that the investor will choose not to hold any active weight in stock A – beyond its weight in the market portfolio – provided that the expected return of the stock precisely compensates the investor for the systematic, undiversifiable exposure to market risk “loaded” onto that stock:
E[Ra]=Rf+βA(E[Rm]–Rf).
Defining the “risk premiums” of stock A and the market, respectively, as their expected returns over-and-above the risk-free rate, we can write this condition compactly as
E[Ra–Rf]=βA(E[Rm]–Rf)⟶ζA=βAζm.
where
ζA=E[Ra]–Rf,ζm=E[Rm]–Rf,βA=σa,mσm2.
For the simple opportunity set that allocates weight to the market portfolio, the risk-free security, and possibly stock A, we can do a bit more tedious arithmetic to get a simple expression for the optimal weight wa. It’s easiest to obtain from the “dual” problem (minimize risk subject to a target return, rather than maximizing return subject to a target level of risk). However one approaches the problem, the key insight is that the weight on stock A will be proportional to the “edge” that investors expect.
κA=ζA–βAζm,
If ζ¯p=E[Rp]–Rf is our target portfolio return over-and-above the risk-free rate. Then
wa=ζ¯pσm2κAD,
where
D=σa2ζm2–2σa,mζaζm+σm2ζa2.
Under standard conditions, D is greater than zero. Therefore, if our objective is to outperform the risk-free return, the weight on stock A will always have the same sign as the “edge” that investors expect it to provide, after taking account of its β-sensitivity to market fluctuations. What matters is not only the expected return of stock A, but also the diversification and “insurance” benefits the security may bring to the overall portfolio.
Investors looking to maximize expected return for some target level of risk will take an “active” position in security A if they believe it will provide them an “edge” – an expected return above what they would normally expect based on its sensitivity to market fluctuations. This is true not only for an individual stock, but for any asset in the “alternative sleeve” of an investment portfolio. For a larger number of assets, one has to consider the whole “matrix” of relationships between portfolio securities, but even the simple 3-security setup above offers a great deal of insight.
If we add the equilibrium condition that all stocks must be held by someone, and if all investors have the same perceptions of expected return and risk, it follows that – in equilibrium – no stock can be “overweighted” or “underweighted” beyond its weight in the “market portfolio”. In that case, all securities lie on the “Security Market Line” defined by the expected return equation above. This is the basic logic behind the “Capital Asset Pricing Model” (CAPM). We certainly don’t need to “believe” the CAPM for our purposes. All that’s important here is to recognize that portfolio overweights and underweights are generally driven by “perceived edge”.
Geek’s Note: Modeling the tech bubble and rising index weights for glamour stocks
Suppose that kh denotes the long-term return that investors expect, in their heads, from the market as a whole, and suppose that stock A is some technology stock that investors believe has sustainable “edge” κA.
It’s sometimes argued that rising weights for technology stocks reflect “inelastic” markets, but that sort of “inelasticity” is really the cost of forcing investors to part with a stock that, in their heads, has high expected returns. The Bubble Term is a direct measure of that reluctance: it’s the gap between the expected returns investors reflect through their trading, and the returns that discounted cash flows can actually deliver. To distinguish the bubble term (which I wrote as β in that August 2025 comment) from market sensitivity, I’ll use the symbol BA for the “bubble term” of stock A in the discussion below.
If wA,t is the market weight of stock A at time t, and investors behave in a way that makes their expectations of high returns self-fulfilling, that weight will evolve as
wA,t+1=wA,t(1+kh+κA)∑jwj,t(1+kh+κj).
Define performance edges so that some stocks are expected to outperform kh, some are expected to underperform kh, but the weighted sum of these edges is zero:
∑jwj,tκj=0.
In that case, the denominator reduces to (1+kh). Define
δ≡11+kh.
It follows that
wA,t+1=wA,tδ(1+kh+κA)=wA,t(1+δκA).
This simply says that if a stock’s perceived “edge” exceeds the expected performance of the average stock, investors will enforce a price trajectory that gives that stock a progressively increasing weight in the market portfolio.
We can extend our bubble model to this individual stock. In investors’ heads, stock A is expected to earn a long-term return of kh+κA, enforced by their own trading behavior. Suppose that stock A throws off deliverable cash flows DA,t that grow at a constant rate gA:
DA,t=DA,0(1+gA)t,
with
kh+κA>gA.
The present value of future cash flows to stock A, discounted at the fundamental rate kf, is
FA,t(kf)=∑j=1∞DA,t+j(1+kf)j=DA,t+1kf–gA,
assuming kf>gA.
Following the same approach I detailed in The Bubble Term, the general solution for the price of stock A then takes exactly the same “fundamental + bubble” form as in the broader market:
PA,t=FA,t(kf)+BA(1+kh+κA)t,BA>0.
Everything in FA,t(kf) can be justified by the future cash flows of stock A, discounted at the fundamental rate kf implied by those cash flows. Everything in the term
BA(1+kh+κA)t
is a “Bubble Term” – a component of the price that must grow at the higher rate kh+κA that investors expect in their heads, even though no additional cash flows provide those returns. The additional “edge” κA that investors expect for stock A implies that the bubble term for this stock grows even faster than the bubble term for the market as a whole. As valuations for glamour technology stocks are driven higher without an offsetting reduction in the expected return that investors hold in their heads, the bubble term BA quietly expands to fill the gap between the returns they investors expect and the returns that the actual future cash flows can plausibly deliver.
Frankly, my impression is that this is precisely what we’re observing in the U.S. stock market. I doubt that it ends well.
